Abstract Methods Results Discussion

Effects of Novel Compound X on Cardiovascular Biomarkers: A Randomised Controlled Trial

Authors: A. Researcher, B. Scientist, C. Analyst

Journal: Journal of Hypothetical Medicine, 2024, Vol. 12, pp. 100–115.

DOI: 10.0000/jhm.2024.12.100

Abstract

Background: Elevated low-density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol remains a primary risk factor for cardiovascular disease, affecting approximately 38% of adults worldwide.

Objective: To assess the efficacy of Compound X (50 mg/day) versus placebo on LDL cholesterol levels over a 12-week intervention period.

Methods: A total of 240 participants (mean age 54.3 ± 7.8 years; 52% female) were randomised 1:1 to receive either Compound X or matching placebo. Fasting lipid panels were obtained at baseline and at weeks 4, 8, and 12.

Results: Compound X reduced LDL cholesterol by 32.4 mg/dL (95% CI: 28.1–36.7 mg/dL) compared with placebo (p < 0.001). Secondary outcomes included a 15.2% reduction in total cholesterol and a non-significant change in HDL cholesterol (−0.8 mg/dL; p = 0.42). Body mass index (BMI) remained stable across both groups (mean 27.6 kg/m² at baseline vs. 27.4 kg/m² at week 12; p = 0.61).

Conclusion: Compound X at 50 mg/day produced a clinically meaningful and statistically significant reduction in LDL cholesterol with a favourable safety profile over 12 weeks.

1. Methods

1.1 Study Design and Participants

This double-blind, placebo-controlled trial enrolled participants aged 40–75 years with baseline LDL cholesterol between 130 mg/dL and 190 mg/dL. Exclusion criteria included current statin use, liver enzyme elevations (> 3× ULN), estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) < 30 mL/min/1.73 m², or pregnancy.

Participants were recruited from 8 clinical sites across three countries between January 2022 and August 2023. Written informed consent was obtained from all participants prior to enrolment. The study was approved by each site's institutional review board (IRB) and registered at ClinicalTrials.gov (NCT00000001).

1.2 Intervention

Eligible participants were randomly assigned in a 1:1 ratio to Compound X (50 mg oral tablet once daily) or matched placebo for 12 weeks. Randomisation was stratified by site and baseline LDL tertile using a permuted block design with block sizes of 4 and 6.

1.3 Outcomes

The primary endpoint was change from baseline in fasting LDL cholesterol (mg/dL) at week 12. Secondary endpoints included percentage change in total cholesterol, HDL cholesterol, triglycerides, and non-HDL cholesterol at weeks 4, 8, and 12. Safety was assessed via adverse event reporting, liver function tests (ALT, AST), and creatinine measurements at each visit.

1.4 Statistical Analysis

Sample size was calculated to detect a minimum difference of 20 mg/dL in LDL reduction between groups, assuming a standard deviation of 35 mg/dL, 80% power, and a two-sided alpha of 0.05. This required 96 participants per group; with anticipated 20% dropout, 120 participants per group were enrolled (total N = 240).

The primary analysis used a mixed-effects model for repeated measures (MMRM) with treatment, visit, treatment-by-visit interaction, and baseline LDL as covariates. All analyses were conducted using Python 3.11 with SciPy 1.11.0 and statsmodels 0.14.0. A two-sided p-value < 0.05 was considered statistically significant.

2. Results

2.1 Participant Characteristics

Of the 240 enrolled participants, 228 (95%) completed the 12-week treatment period. Baseline characteristics were well balanced between groups (Table 1). Mean baseline LDL cholesterol was 158.3 ± 18.6 mg/dL in the Compound X group and 157.9 ± 19.2 mg/dL in the placebo group.

Table 1. Baseline Participant Characteristics
Characteristic Compound X (n = 120) Placebo (n = 120)
Age, years (mean ± SD) 54.1 ± 7.6 54.5 ± 8.0
Female, n (%) 63 (52.5%) 62 (51.7%)
BMI, kg/m² (mean ± SD) 27.6 ± 4.1 27.5 ± 4.3
LDL cholesterol, mg/dL (mean ± SD) 158.3 ± 18.6 157.9 ± 19.2
Total cholesterol, mg/dL (mean ± SD) 221.4 ± 24.7 220.8 ± 25.1
HDL cholesterol, mg/dL (mean ± SD) 48.2 ± 10.3 47.9 ± 10.8
Triglycerides, mg/dL (mean ± SD) 142.6 ± 55.4 145.1 ± 57.2

2.2 Primary Outcome

At week 12, participants in the Compound X group experienced a mean reduction in LDL cholesterol of 32.4 mg/dL (95% CI: 28.1–36.7 mg/dL) from baseline, compared with a mean reduction of 1.8 mg/dL (95% CI: −1.2 to 4.8 mg/dL) in the placebo group. The between-group difference was −30.6 mg/dL (95% CI: −35.1 to −26.1 mg/dL; p < 0.001).

The effect was apparent as early as week 4, with Compound X reducing LDL by 21.3 mg/dL (95% CI: 17.8–24.8 mg/dL; p < 0.001) relative to placebo at that time point.

2.3 Secondary Outcomes

Total cholesterol was reduced by 15.2% in the Compound X group versus 0.9% in the placebo group (p < 0.001). Non-HDL cholesterol decreased by 29.8 mg/dL (SD 14.2 mg/dL) with Compound X compared with 1.3 mg/dL (SD 9.7 mg/dL) with placebo (p < 0.001).

Triglycerides showed a modest but significant reduction of 12.4 mg/dL in the Compound X group (p = 0.03). HDL cholesterol did not differ significantly between groups (Δ −0.8 mg/dL; p = 0.42).

2.4 Safety

Treatment-emergent adverse events (TEAEs) occurred in 34 participants (28.3%) receiving Compound X and 31 participants (25.8%) receiving placebo (p = 0.65). No serious adverse events were attributed to Compound X. Mean ALT levels increased by 3.2 U/L in the Compound X group (within normal range; reference < 40 U/L), compared with 0.5 U/L in the placebo group (p = 0.08). No participant discontinued due to liver enzyme elevations.

3. Discussion

Our findings demonstrate that Compound X at 50 mg/day produces a clinically meaningful reduction in LDL cholesterol of 32.4 mg/dL over 12 weeks—substantially exceeding the pre-specified minimum clinically important difference of 20 mg/dL. The magnitude of effect (approximately 20.5% relative reduction from a baseline of 158.3 mg/dL) is comparable to low-to-moderate intensity statin therapy.

Secondary lipid endpoints further support the beneficial lipid-modifying profile of Compound X. A 15.2% reduction in total cholesterol and a 12.4 mg/dL decrease in triglycerides are consistent with a multi-pathway mechanism of action. The absence of a significant effect on HDL cholesterol (p = 0.42) is common among LDL-targeting agents and does not detract from the overall favourable profile.

The safety profile was reassuring. The overall TEAE rate of 28.3% with Compound X compared with 25.8% with placebo (p = 0.65) indicates no statistically significant increase in adverse events. The small, non-significant rise in ALT (3.2 U/L; p = 0.08) will be monitored in future longer-term studies.

Limitations include the 12-week follow-up duration, which precludes assessment of hard cardiovascular outcomes, and the relatively homogeneous study population. A larger phase III trial with 2-year follow-up is currently planned (target N = 3000; estimated completion 2027).

References

  1. Smith A, Jones B. LDL cholesterol and cardiovascular risk: a meta-analysis of 42 studies (n = 185,000 participants). Lancet Cardiol. 2020;8(3):210–219.
  2. Brown C, et al. Compound X phase I safety and pharmacokinetics in 60 healthy volunteers. Clin Pharmacol Ther. 2021;110(4):987–995.
  3. World Heart Federation. Global cardiovascular disease fact sheet, 2023. Available at: https://world-heart-federation.org. Accessed 1 January 2024.
